did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a previously validated artificial intelligence-based algorithm utilizing a convolutional neural network (CNN) to automatically assess and grade the PRO score of actinic keratosis (AK) lesions using line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) images. The AI system was used to obtain results regarding the grading of AK severity and its agreement with expert visual assessments.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article presents original experimental results from a retrospective study evaluating an AI-based algorithm on a cohort of 100 histologically confirmed actinic keratosis lesions. It includes specific sections for Materials and Methods, Results (with statistical analysis, Cohen's kappa, and confidence intervals), and Discussion, indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Innovation in Actinic Keratosis Assessment: Artificial Intelligence-Based Approach to LC-OCT PRO Score Evaluation
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of a previously validated artificial intelligence-based algorithm using a convolutional neural network to automatically assess the PRO score of actinic keratosis (AK) lesions from line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) images. The AI system was tested on a dataset of 19,898 LC-OCT images derived from 80 histologically confirmed AK lesions. The main evaluation approach involved comparing the AI-automated PRO grading against visual assessments by imaging experts, calculating agreement rates and weighted kappa statistics. The results showed that AI-automated average PRO grading agreed with expert visual scores in 71.3% of cases, with a weighted kappa of 0.51. The study concluded that the AI-based approach is a reliable, objective, and significantly faster tool for optimizing the efficiency and accuracy of AK diagnosis compared to traditional visual assessment.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on 80 histologically confirmed actinic keratosis lesions. The AI algorithm was trained and tested to grade the severity of these AK lesions using the PRO score system (PRO I-III) based on LC-OCT images.

Mentioned as the invasive progression target of AKs and for histological comparison (e.g., loss of DEJ in invasive SCC vs AK). Not the primary target of the AI grading experiment, which focused on AK severity.

all_task_types.target: PRO score grading (PRO I, PRO II, PRO III)
all_task_types.details: The AI algorithm classifies actinic keratosis lesions into discrete severity grades (PRO I, PRO II, PRO III) based on LC-OCT images. The study evaluates agreement between AI-automated PRO grading and expert visual scores using contingency tables, sensitivity, specificity, and Cohen's weighted kappa.

datasets_used.name: Primary Study Cohort (LC-OCT AK Lesions)
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of patients with histologically confirmed actinic keratoses recruited from the Department of Dermatology and Allergology at the University Hospital Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU) in Munich and the University Hospital in Augsburg between December 2019 and December 2021. The dataset consists of line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) images acquired using the deepLive device.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the previously validated AI-based algorithm by comparing AI-automated PRO grading against expert visual scores.
use.other: Ground truth for visual PRO score assessment was obtained from the consensus of two dermatologists and non-invasive imaging experts.

datasets_used.name: AI Training Dataset (from previous pilot study)
datasets_used.description: A database of LC-OCT images used to train the convolutional neural network (CNN) algorithm prior to this study. Referenced from a previous pilot study [22].

use.training: Used to train the AI-based algorithm to automatically score the presence and severity of AKs.
use.other: The algorithm was previously validated on this dataset before being applied to the primary study cohort.

statistical_methods_used.method: Cohen’s weighted kappa coefficient with linear weights
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate the correlation/agreement between the LC-OCT AI PRO score and the visual expert PRO score for both average and maximum grading.
To provide uncertainty estimates for the Cohen's weighted kappa coefficient and Spearman correlation coefficients.

statistical_methods_used.method: Sensitivity, Specificity, Positive Predictive Value (PPV), Negative Predictive Value (NPV)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the accuracy of the AI-based assessment and the PRO score scoring system.
To report the agreement between LC-OCT AI PRO score and the visual expert PRO score.
To measure the correlation between the visual PRO score and continuous metrics (undulation index, number of protrusions, maximum depth of protrusion).
To determine if the increase in undulation index, number of protrusions, and maximum depth of protrusion in relation to the growth of the LC-OCT expert visual PRO score was statistically significant.
To determine statistical significance for the t-test and kappa coefficient results.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant increase in the undulation index, number of protrusions, and maximum depth of protrusion in relation to the growth of the LC-OCT expert visual PRO score.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant increase in the undulation index, number of protrusions, and maximum depth of protrusion in relation to the growth of the LC-OCT expert visual PRO score.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The increase in undulation index, number of protrusions, and maximum depth of protrusion in relation to the growth of the LC-OCT expert visual PRO score was shown to be statistically significant (p < 0.05).

error_analysis.specific_errors: PRO I: 7/21 overestimated as PRO II; PRO II: 10/48 underestimated as PRO I; PRO III: 6/11 underestimated as PRO II, none as PRO I

average_PRO_grading.notes: The AI automated average grading agreed with expert visual grading in 57/80 (71%) of the cases.

maximum_PRO_grading.notes: Performance showed a lower correlation than the average grading score.

continuous_metrics_correlation.notes: Continuous increase in undulation index, number of protrusions, and maximum depth of protrusion in relation to the growth of the LC-OCT expert visual PRO score was statistically significant (t-test, p < 0.05).

sensitivity.notes: Reported in the Discussion section as a primary advantage of AI-based assessment.

AI Training Dataset (from previous pilot.description: Database of 458 images extracted from 104 AK lesions of 59 patients
AI Training Dataset (from previous pilot.notes: The algorithm was previously validated on this dataset. Specific quantitative results for this training set are not reported in the current study's results section, only referenced as the basis for the CNN training.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on a database of 458 LC-OCT images extracted from 104 AK lesions of 59 patients (referenced from previous pilot study [22]).
architecture_used.input_size: Vertical slice images (single images, videos acquired in vertical slice mode, or vertical projection of 3D stacks). Image size: 1.2 × 0.5 mm², lateral and axial resolution: 1.1 µm × 1.3 µm.

architecture_details.function: Identify and classify features in LC-OCT images to generate a PRO score for each image.
architecture_details.processing: Works on vertical slice images; provides a PRO score for each image. Average lesion PRO score is calculated as the average of all image scores. Maximum lesion PRO score is calculated as the maximum score present in at least 10% of the images.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: PRO score (PRO I, PRO II, PRO III)
classification_or_output_head.aggregation_method: Average or Maximum (top 10%) of per-image scores

preprocessing_techniques: Manual exclusion of images with insufficient quality for visual assessment (e.g., lesions with thick hyperkeratosis on all images or inappropriate image acquisition)

Exclusion of images with strong artifacts preventing accurate segmentation of the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ), such as air bubbles, black images at the end of the video, or 3D images with significant motion artifacts
Selection of the most representative LC-OCT acquisition (single images, videos, or 3D blocks) for each lesion by two dermatologists and non-invasive imaging experts
Processing of vertical slice images (single images, videos acquired in vertical slice mode, or vertical projection of 3D stacks) for the AI algorithm input
conclusions: The AI-automated average PRO grading showed high agreement with expert visual scores in 71.3% of cases (57/80), with a weighted kappa of 0.51 (p < 0.001, 95% CI [0.24, 0.68]).
The AI-based assessment was significantly faster than regular visual PRO score assessment, optimizing the efficiency of AK diagnosis.

AI-automated maximum PRO grading (based on the maximum score present in at least 10% of images) showed lower correlation with expert visual scores, with an agreement of 54% (43/80) and a weighted kappa of 0.26 (p < 0.001, 95% CI [-0.02, 0.35]).

The AI algorithm demonstrated a coherent correlation between visual PRO scores and continuous metrics: undulation index (ρ: 0.434), number of protrusions (ρ: 0.434), and maximum depth of protrusion (ρ: 0.53), all statistically significant (p < 0.05).
The study confirms that the AI-based grading of LC-OCT images is a reliable and fast tool to optimize the efficiency of visual PRO score grading.
This non-invasive and fast AI-based approach has the potential to improve the accuracy and speed of AK diagnosis, leading to better clinical outcomes for patients.
important_remarks: The study evaluated a previously validated AI algorithm on a retrospective cohort of 80 histologically confirmed AK lesions (19,898 LC-OCT images) from two German university hospitals, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The AI algorithm was trained on a small, distinct dataset of 458 images from 104 lesions (59 patients) in a prior pilot study [22], raising potential concerns about dataset shift or overfitting if the training distribution differs significantly from the test cohort.

The AI-automated average PRO grading showed only moderate agreement with expert visual scores (71.3% agreement, weighted kappa = 0.51), indicating significant room for improvement in diagnostic accuracy.

The AI-automated maximum PRO grading performed poorly (54% agreement, weighted kappa = 0.26), suggesting the 'max 10%' aggregation method is less robust than the average method for this specific task.
There was a notable class imbalance in the test set, with only 11 lesions classified as PRO III, which may limit the statistical power and reliability of performance metrics for severe lesions.

The study excluded images with insufficient quality or strong artifacts (e.g., thick hyperkeratosis, air bubbles), which may introduce selection bias by excluding difficult-to-diagnose cases that are common in clinical practice.

The AI system's sensitivity was reported as 71.3%, but specific sensitivity/specificity values for individual PRO grades were not explicitly detailed in the results summary, limiting granular performance assessment.
The data are not publicly available due to ethical and privacy restrictions, preventing independent validation or reproducibility of the study's findings.

The study acknowledges that AKs exhibit histological diversity within a single lesion, and the AI's reliance on LC-OCT images (which may not capture the entire lesion depth or extent as accurately as histology) is a limitation compared to the gold standard.
